---
title: "My ISPOR 2026 Experience: What I Learned & Tips to Make the Most of It"
date: "2026-05-28"
categories: [HEOR, RWE, Conference, Career]
description: "Attended ISPOR 2026 in Philadelphia. Here's what I took away and what I'd tell anyone heading there next time."
image: ""
draft: false
---

I attended my first ISPOR 2026 conference in Philadelphia. I enrolled in two short courses and attended one main conference day. It was a lot, in the best way. Here's what I took away and what I'd tell anyone heading there next time.

---

## Go in with a clear goal

ISPOR is massive. Without a goal, it's easy to wander. Before you go, identify the area you want to grow in, whether that's real-world evidence, comparative effectiveness, causal inference, whatever it is. That focus shapes everything: which sessions you attend, which booths you visit, which conversations you seek out.

For me, I'm actively building toward HEOR and RWE, so I chose my short courses around that. I attended:

- **Causal Inference and Causal Diagrams in Big, Real-World Observational Data and Pragmatic Trials**
- **Developing Decision-Grade Real-World Evidence**

Both aligned directly with where I want to grow and being in a room full of people working on the same problems made it immediately clear who I wanted to talk to. That's the point. Your goal doesn't have to be complicated, just intentional.

---

## Network intentionally, not aggressively

Set a realistic target — 3 to 5 genuine conversations. Not just LinkedIn connections. Conversations.

**Poster sessions are underrated for this.** Find research that interests you, walk up, ask the presenter about their work. People love talking about their research. Ask if they're open to collaboration. Connect on LinkedIn. That's it.

If you're interested in a specific area, say, comparative effectiveness — browse the poster presentations beforehand, find what genuinely fascinates you, and make a plan to talk to those presenters. It's a natural, low-pressure way to start a real conversation.

If you're interested in a company, visit their booth, ideally in the morning before it gets crowded. Ask for methodology folks if you want to understand their tools and approach. Ask for HR if you're exploring opportunities. Be direct, be friendly.

---

## Plan before you arrive

Download the ISPOR app — it's genuinely useful. Before the conference:

- Finalize your short courses and plenary sessions
- Browse the attendee list and flag interesting profiles
- Review the exhibitor list and make a priority ordered list of booths to visit

Keep only your planned sessions in the app so you're not distracted scrolling through everything else.

---

## Use your in-person time wisely

Most sessions are recorded. Your competitive advantage at the conference is being *there* hands-on workshops, live debates, poster conversations, hallway chats. Prioritize the things that don't translate to a recording.

---

## Practical stuff that actually matters

- **Wear comfortable shoes.** Seriously. The walking is brutal.
- **Bring protein bars and water.** You'll thank yourself.
- **Don't overdo it.** One focused day beats three burned-out ones.
- **Talk to people.** Even just a hi. Everyone is approachable. You never know where a conversation leads.

---

Hopefully this helps. Keep it simple, planned, and focused - that's what worked for me. :)
